Swirling currents.<[silence:40ms]> Trash’s final resting place.<[silence:40ms]>
The term gyre comes from the Greek gyros—meaning "circle"—because these vast ocean loops spin like slow-motion whirlpools, trapping anything that floats.<[silence:40ms]>

Five major gyres dominate the planet:<[silence:40ms]> the North Pacific, South Pacific, North Atlantic, South Atlantic, and Indian Ocean.<[silence:40ms]> Each acts like a cosmic laundry machine, churning water in loops thousands of kilometers wide.<[silence:40ms]> Plastic bottles, fishing nets, and microbeads get caught in their grip, pulled toward the calm center where winds die down.<[silence:40ms]> The North Pacific Gyre holds the most infamous trap—the Great Pacific Garbage Patch—a floating landfill twice the size of Texas, yet invisible from satellites because it’s mostly confetti-sized debris suspended meters deep.<[silence:40ms]>

clean_story:
Swirling currents. Trash’s final resting place.
The term gyre comes from the Greek gyros—meaning "circle"—because these vast ocean loops spin like slow-motion whirlpools, trapping anything that floats.

Five major gyres dominate the planet: the North Pacific, South Pacific, North Atlantic, South Atlantic, and Indian Ocean. Each acts like a cosmic laundry machine, churning water in loops thousands of kilometers wide. Plastic bottles, fishing nets, and microbeads get caught in their grip, pulled toward the calm center where winds die down. The North Pacific Gyre holds the most infamous trap—the Great Pacific Garbage Patch—a floating landfill twice the size of Texas, yet invisible from satellites because it’s mostly confetti-sized debris suspended meters deep.

Here’s the twist: gyres don’t just collect trash; they shred it. Sunlight and waves break plastic into microscopic bits, turning the patch into a toxic soup. Albatrosses mistake colorful fragments for squid, feeding them to chicks whose stomachs fill with indigestible plastic. Even plankton, the ocean’s foundation, ingest microplastics, passing them up the food chain to fish—and eventually, our plates.

No cleanup crew exists. The gyres are too vast, the debris too dispersed. The best fix? Stop the flow at the source.

Three related subjects are plastic-eating enzymes, deep-sea pollution currents, and how jellyfish outlast trash.
